text:
# Cloud power lies outside Europe for the time being

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html)

20/05/2025 • 4 minutes reading

[Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/julia-krauwer/index.html)

The European cloud market is dominated by American technology companies. Local players hardly exceed a few percent market share. However, there is a growing realization that Europe itself must gain control over crucial digital services.

A majority of European governments and companies run on cloud infrastructure of American origin: Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud. Together these parties control about 70 to 80 percent of the European [cloud market](https://sciencebusiness.net/news/ict/new-franco-german-cloud-computing-project-set-standards-and-challenge-us-market-leaders#:~:text=However%2C%20with%20almost%2070%20per,for%20Cyber%20Studies%20%26%20Research). The rest is fragmented among dozens of smaller providers, including European parties such as SAP, Deutsche Telekom, OVHcloud and Orange. However, no European player achieves more than a few percent market share. SAP and Deutsche Telekom top the European rankings with each only [2 percent of the market](https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/european-cloud-providers-continue-to-grow-but-still-lose-market-share#:~:text=been%20Amazon%2C%20Microsoft%20and%20Google,list%20of%20national%20and%20regional).

## Applying pressure through IT

metadata.source_title: Cloud power lies outside Europe for the time being

text:
## Applying pressure through IT

The Dutch government also appears to be highly dependent on the cloud services of the American tech giants. Almost half (44 percent) of the cloud services used by the government fall into the 'public cloud' category and therefore rely on the infrastructure of these parties. In many cases, a risk assessment has not been done, as appears from a recent report from the Court of Audit with the ominous subtitle Dark clouds are gathering. According to Simon Besteman of the Dutch Cloud Community trade association, the risks are substantial. “If your data is in a data center of a cloud provider with an American UBO, the American government can access it. It doesn't matter if the data center is in Europe.”

The trade association warns that technological dependence puts countries and companies in a dire position, especially if geopolitical relations deteriorate. For example, Amsterdam Trade Bank (ATB), a subsidiary of the Russian Alfa Bank, was cut off from IT services by Amazon and Microsoft after the invasion of Ukraine, resulting in major disruptions: employees lost access to applications and e-mail, digital payments came to a standstill. It fuels fears of a scenario in which American tech companies discontinue their services to Europe if political pressure increases. “If the United States wants to put pressure on us, this can be done through IT,” says Besteman.

## European providers are losing market share

US clouds have been growing steadily in recent years, even faster than local providers. Synergy Research calculated that the European cloud market increased five times between 2017 and 2022, but the combined market share of European providers fell from 27 percent to 13 percent. Amazon, Microsoft and Google invest billions every quarter in new European data centers.

European providers are therefore retreating to niches: they serve specific local needs or act as a partner of the big three in hybrid constructions. For example, the French OVHcloud provides cloud services that comply with strict European regulations. In some cases, organizations combine these services with those of Amazon Web Services or Microsoft Azure, allowing them to take advantage of the scale and functionality of US platforms, while keeping sensitive data within Europe and subject to European rules.

metadata.source_title: Cloud power lies outside Europe for the time being

text:
The dominance of American cloud providers extends to the backbone of artificial intelligence: the advanced servers on which AI models are trained and run. The United States has about [70 percent](https://geopolitique.eu/en/2025/02/10/financing-infrastructure-for-a-competitive-european-ai/#:~:text=A%20risk%20of%20strategic%20dependence,higher%20than%20in%20the%20US.) of this global computing power for AI, the vast majority of which – 80 percent – is in the hands of the hyperscalers. Europe has to make do with only 4 percent of the global capacity.

## National cloud under own management

Yet there is movement. There is a growing realization within Europe on several fronts that technological autonomy is only feasible with substantial investments, smart regulations and local alternatives. “The question for the Dutch government is no longer whether we should reduce dependence on American cloud companies, but how,” says Besteman of the Dutch Cloud Community. The House of Representatives recently adopted a motion calling on the government to put out a tender for a “government cloud under full Dutch management”.

Besteman does not expect such a government cloud to be created overnight, but such an intention could set the local cloud market in motion. “If you want European cloud providers to invest further in their services, there must first be a buying signal.” Similar discussions are now also taking place in the business world, Besteman sees. “They also consider how safe their data actually is, and what a plan B could be, an exit strategy.”

## Network of providers

From Europe, the focus is not so much on one alternative cloud solution that can compete with Microsoft Azure or Amazon Web Services in terms of scale, but on a network of providers under which data and workloads can easily be moved and distributed. ECOFED is an example of such a 'federated cloud'. The Dutch project is developing a solution that allows software and physical cloud infrastructure to be decoupled, giving users more freedom in choosing and combining cloud services.

metadata.source_title: Cloud power lies outside Europe for the time being

text:
The Dutch LeaseWeb is also making itself heard. In March, the cloud provider presented the first results of its contribution to the IPCEI-CIS project – the same initiative that includes ECOFED. The company is building a cloud platform that is specially designed to comply with European laws and regulations. The platform forms the heart of LeaseWeb's so-called European Cloud Campus – a broader investment in sovereign cloud technology, local data centers and collaboration with knowledge institutions.

Complete sovereignty seems unrealistic, but there is a growing realization that Europe needs to gain more control over its digital infrastructure. Exit strategies and alternative scenarios are given a more emphatic place on the agenda of government and business. This offers room for local cloud companies to distinguish themselves with security and compliance as an asset.

[Read also: Europe focuses on technological self-reliance](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/europa-zet-op-technologische-zelfreligigheid.html)

## Read more in the technology sector

Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest growing sectors. Aided by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector is the basis for the digital innovation of other sectors.
